summary: DeVault explains C pointers from first principles, starting with how memory works as a flat array of octets and building up through pointer declaration, dereferencing, multi-byte values, and indirect pointers. He covers void pointers, structure dereferencing, pointer arithmetic, and NULL pointers. The post originated from helping a new Sway contributor who was learning C and encountered void** in the codebase. The explanation uses a simplified 16-bit system model to make pointer mechanics concrete and visual.
key_points: Pointers are fixed-size integers representing memory addresses, and all pointers are the same size regardless of what type they reference
Pointer arithmetic scales by the size of the pointed-to type, which is why array indexing and pointer addition are equivalent in C
Void pointers strip away type information, storing only an address that can be cast to any specific pointer type for dereferencing
Multi-level indirection (pointer to pointer) follows the same logic recursively, with each dereference following one level of addressing
money_quotes: Your RAM is basically a flat array of octets.
All pointers are the same size.
A void* stores an arbitrary address without bringing along any type information.
++ on a pointer actually adds sizeof(type) to the actual address stored.
The idea is that if we only added one, we'd be referring to an address that is in the middle of a uint16_t, rather than the next uint16_t in memory that we meant to refer to.
Dereferencing a NULL pointer is generally a Bad Thing and will lead to segfaults.
key_insight: Pointers are simply fixed-size integers that hold memory addresses, and every pointer operation—dereferencing, arithmetic, indirection—follows logically from understanding memory as a flat array of bytes.
